course.title: EV Awareness & Safety
course.subtitle: Master the fundamentals of electric vehicle identification, high-voltage hazards, safety equipment, emergency response, and charging procedures.

components.body: Your essential guide to identifying electric vehicles, understanding high-voltage hazards, and working safely in the modern automotive workplace.
components.imagePrompt: Modern electric vehicle in a sleek automotive workshop, dramatic lighting with electric blue accent glow, dark atmospheric background, cinematic wide-angle composition, professional photography, high resolution, no text

_graphic.alt: Electric vehicle in a modern workshop with dramatic blue lighting

components.body:
This training course is designed to improve your knowledge of Electric Vehicles (EVs) and make you aware of the associated risks, so that you may exercise appropriate care for yourself and others when working near an Electric Vehicle.

Important: This first-level online course does not qualify you to work directly on high-voltage systems. You should inform your colleagues of your current trained level and request further assistance from a suitably trained person when necessary.

components.instruction: Explore each objective to understand what you'll learn.

_items.title: Identify Electric & Hybrid Vehicles
_items.body: Know about the types of electric and hybrid vehicles currently available. State how to identify electric/hybrid vehicles and give examples of the electric/hybrid vehicles that are currently available.

_items.title: Understand High-Voltage Hazards
_items.body: Understand the hazards around motor vehicle high energy electrical systems. State the hazards associated with high energy electricity and those present when connecting and charging electric/hybrid vehicles.

_items.title: Work Safely Around EVs
_items.body: Know how to work safely around electric/hybrid vehicles. State safety precautions to be taken before approaching and working around electric/hybrid vehicles. Identify high energy cabling and components.

_items.title: Safe Charging Procedures
_items.body: Understand how vehicles may be safely charged using an external source. Know the correct procedures and terminology for EV charging systems.

components.body: Please enter your details before continuing.

It is important that you can recognise an electric vehicle when it comes into your workplace. You will then be ready to exercise the correct procedures necessary for a safe visit.

Identifying features will differ between manufacturers. This means you may need to exercise your skill and experience to determine the vehicle type — getting it wrong could be dangerous.

Look for these key exterior features:

  • Number plate: Full EVs may have a green border (not mandatory)
  • Badging: Many EVs display a badge indicating electric power
  • Charge port: BEVs and PHEVs have a battery charge connector port on the vehicle exterior
components.imagePrompt: Close-up of electric vehicle exterior showing green number plate border and EV badge, modern car in workshop, professional photography, dark background with blue accent lighting, 800x600, no text

_graphic.alt: Electric vehicle exterior showing green number plate and charging port

components.body:
When inspecting the engine bay, look for these indicators:

  • Orange high-voltage cables — the universal indicator of an EV electrical system
  • Power controller / inverter — converts battery DC to motor AC
  • Warning labels — high-voltage warnings on components and covers
components.imagePrompt: EV engine bay close-up showing bright orange high-voltage cables power inverter and warning labels, technical automotive photography, dark moody lighting, 800x600, no text

_graphic.alt: EV engine bay with orange high-voltage cables and warning labels

components.body: You can recognise an Electric Vehicle by looking for these identifying criteria. Not all types of EV will have the same identifiers.
components.instruction: Review each indicator you would check when identifying an EV.

_items.text: Badging on exterior bodywork
_items.detail: Look for EV, Electric, e-tron, or similar manufacturer badges.

_items.text: External mains charging connection port
_items.detail: Present on BEV and PHEV vehicles — not on standard HEV.

_items.text: Instrument cluster warnings and messages
_items.detail: Dashboard displays showing EV status and high-voltage warnings.

_items.text: Power flow / energy use screen on dashboard
_items.detail: Shows real-time energy flow between battery, motor, and wheels.

_items.text: Bright-coloured (orange) high-voltage cables
_items.detail: The universal indicator of high-voltage systems in any EV.

_items.text: Green band on number plate
_items.detail: Not mandatory — do not rely on this alone.

_items.text: Warning labels on HV components
_items.detail: Yellow and black high-voltage warning triangles on covers and components.

components.body: Not all types of EV will have the same identifying features. A Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) may not have a charging port, but it still contains high-voltage components that present serious risks.
Understanding the differences between vehicle types is essential for assessing risk and applying the correct procedures.

_items.title: BEV — Battery Electric Vehicle
_items.body: Fully electric, powered entirely by a rechargeable battery pack. No internal combustion engine. Charged exclusively via external mains connection. Voltage range: 350-800V.
_items.imagePrompt: Modern battery electric vehicle BEV sleek design blue accent lighting professional automotive photography dark background 600x400 no text

_items.title: HEV — Hybrid Electric Vehicle
_items.body: Combines a conventional engine with an electric motor. The battery charges through regenerative braking — there is no external charging port. Voltage range: 200-400V.
_items.imagePrompt: Hybrid electric vehicle cutaway showing dual powertrain engine and electric motor dark background with blue highlights 600x400 no text

_items.title: PHEV — Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle
_items.body: Has both a combustion engine and an electric motor with a larger battery than HEV. Can be charged via external mains connection for extended electric-only range. Voltage range: 200-450V.
_items.imagePrompt: Plug-in hybrid vehicle charging at modern charging station illuminated charge port dark atmospheric setting 600x400 no text

components.body: Different vehicle types can be identified by different features. Use this table as a quick reference.
You can use the UK Government Vehicle Enquiry website to check a vehicle's fuel type using its registration number. Which of the following is the correct URL?

_items.text: vehicleenquiry.service.gov.uk
dvla.gov.uk/vehicle-check
mot-check.service.gov.uk

_feedback.correct: Correct! The Government Vehicle Enquiry service at vehicleenquiry.service.gov.uk allows you to check a vehicle's fuel type.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. The correct URL is vehicleenquiry.service.gov.uk

components.body: High-voltage systems in EVs present unique risks that require specific knowledge and precautions.
components.imagePrompt: High-voltage warning signs and orange cables in automotive workshop dramatic lighting with warning amber and electric blue accents cinematic wide shot dark atmospheric 1920x800 no text

_graphic.alt: High-voltage warning signs and orange cables in automotive workshop

components.body:
Electric and hybrid vehicles introduce hazards into the workplace in addition to those normally associated with vehicle repair and maintenance.

Voltages present in EVs are significantly higher — currently up to 650 Volts DC — compared to conventional vehicles (12/24V DC). In dry conditions, accidental contact with parts live at voltages above 110V DC can be fatal.

Battery systems may contain chemicals that can be harmful if released. They also store significant amounts of energy that can give rise to explosion if not dealt with correctly.

components.displayTitle: Key Risks of Working with EVs
components.instruction: Select each risk category to learn more.

_items.title: Fatal Electric Shock
_items.body: The presence of high-voltage components and cabling capable of delivering a fatal electric shock. Components may retain dangerous voltage even when the vehicle is switched off.

_items.title: Fire & Explosion Risk
_items.body: The storage of electrical energy with the potential to cause explosion or fire. Stored electrical energy can be sufficient to cause arc flash — a sudden release of energy through the air.

_items.title: Silent Operation
_items.body: The possibility of people being unaware of vehicles moving, as when electrically driven they are silent in operation. Most EVs have AVAS (Acoustic Vehicle Alerting System), but the sound may not be what you expect.

_items.title: Unexpected Movement
_items.body: Electric motors or the vehicle itself may move unexpectedly due to magnetic forces within the motors. Vehicles can be ready to drive without a running engine.

_items.title: Battery Chemical Hazards
_items.body: The potential for release of explosive gases and harmful liquids and chemicals if batteries are damaged or incorrectly modified. Manual handling risks are also associated with battery replacement.

_items.title: Medical Device Interference
_items.body: The potential for the electrical systems on the vehicle to affect medical devices such as pacemakers. Personnel with medical implants should exercise additional caution.

_items.definition: The UK Health and Safety Executive provides specific guidance for working with electric and hybrid vehicles in the motor vehicle repair industry. Source: hse.gov.uk/mvr/topics/electric-hybrid.htm
A UN regulation that defines 'High Voltage' as >60V to ≤1500V DC (or >30V to ≤1000V AC). Specifies all required safety tests for EV approval.
The UK HSE identifies 4 categories of EV work: valeting/sales (low risk), incident response, routine maintenance (excluding HV), and working on high-voltage electrical systems (specialist only).

components.body: Even for valeting, sales, and other lower-risk activities around EVs, these precautions must be followed:

_items.title: Secure the Key Fob
_items.body: Keep remote operation keys away from the vehicle to prevent accidental power-up. Use a signal-blocking pouch if available.

_items.title: Be Aware of Silent Movement
_items.body: People moving EVs around the workplace should be aware that others may not hear them approaching. Sound your horn when necessary.

_items.title: Avoid Pressure Washing HV Components
_items.body: High-pressure water can damage high-voltage electrical components and cables. Orange cables indicate HV systems — refer to manufacturer guidance before valeting.

_items.title: Never Touch Orange Cables
_items.body: Orange cables carry high voltage. Never handle, move, or lean tools against them. Report any visible damage immediately.

components.body:
A voltage as low as 50 volts applied between two parts of the human body causes a current to flow that can block the electrical signals between the brain and the muscles.

This may cause:

  • Stopping the heart beating properly
  • Preventing the person from breathing
  • Causing muscle spasms
  • Secondary injuries such as burns and fall injuries
components.imagePrompt: Medical illustration showing effects of electric shock on human body stylised with electric blue current paths dark background professional medical style 800x600 no text

_graphic.alt: Effects of electric shock on the human body

components.body: The severity of an electric shock depends on a combination of factors:

_items.title: Voltage Level
_items.body: EV systems operate at up to 650V DC — far exceeding the 50V threshold for danger. Higher voltage drives more current through the body.

_items.title: Body Resistance
_items.body: Dry skin offers more resistance than wet skin. Non-conductive materials reduce current flow, while high-conductivity materials increase it significantly.

_items.title: Current Path
_items.body: Finger-to-finger (same hand) is less dangerous than hand-to-foot or across the chest, which can pass through the heart.

_items.title: Duration of Contact
_items.body: Even milliseconds of contact can cause injury. One second or more of sustained contact with high voltage is frequently fatal.

components.body: If you encounter someone receiving an electric shock, follow these steps in order:

_nodes.title: Assess the Situation
_nodes.body: Do not touch the casualty if they are still in contact with the electrical source — you are at risk of electrocution.

_nodes.title: Isolate the Power Source
_nodes.body: For mains chargers, turn off power at the isolation switch. For vehicles, the HV supply should disconnect when turning off with the key — but consider there may be a fault.

_nodes.title: Separate Casualty from Source
_nodes.body: Use a purpose-designed insulated rescue hook. Do not attempt untested rescue methods if you are not trained.

_nodes.title: Call 999
_nodes.body: Call emergency services immediately. Specify this is a high-voltage electrical incident.

_nodes.title: Provide First Aid
_nodes.body: Once contact is broken, perform a primary survey and treat injuries. Seek assistance from qualified first aid personnel.

_nodes.title: Secure the Scene
_nodes.body: Ensure no one else works on the vehicle until made safe. Cordon off with non-conductive barrier chain.

components.body: When electric vehicles enter your workplace, specific safety equipment must be available and correctly used. Test your knowledge of each piece of equipment below.

_items.title: Vehicle ID Sign
_items.body: Should be present on every electric vehicle entering a workshop for repair so that all staff are aware it is an EV.

_items.title: Barrier Chain
_items.body: A non-conductive safety barrier used to alert others and prevent unauthorised access to the vehicle work area.

_items.title: Key Signal Blocker
_items.body: A protective pouch that blocks wireless signals from car keys, preventing accidental power-up on keyless-start vehicles.

_items.title: Rescue Hook
_items.body: An insulated rescue hook for trained staff to remove a casualty from an electric shock source. Must be kept visible and within easy reach.

_items.title: Insulated Gloves
_items.body: Class 0 rated gloves for up to 1500V DC. For suitably trained persons working on HV systems only.

_items.title: Warning Signs
_items.body: Warning and status signs that alert others to risks present. Includes 'Do Not Start / Work in Progress' signs.

components.body: Select the primary purpose for the vehicle identification sign.

_items.text: Tells staff that the vehicle might need charging
Should be present on every electric vehicle entering a workshop so all staff are aware it is an EV
Allows the service advisor to find their cars easily

_feedback.correct: Correct! The vehicle ID sign ensures all staff know an EV is present.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. The sign's primary purpose is to alert all staff that the vehicle is an EV.

components.body: Select the primary purpose for the barrier chain / cordon.

_items.text: This is used to hang the signs on the vehicle
Stops anyone moving the vehicle from the workbay
Alerts others and prevents unauthorised access

_feedback.correct: Correct! The barrier chain alerts others and prevents unauthorised access.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. The barrier chain's primary purpose is to alert others and prevent unauthorised access.

components.body: Select the primary purpose for the key signal blocker pouch.

_items.text: Prevents the key from being stolen
Prevents accidental power-up on keyless-start vehicles by blocking key signals
Stops the key from being lost

_feedback.correct: Correct! The signal blocker prevents accidental power-up by blocking key signals.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. The pouch blocks key signals to prevent accidental vehicle power-up.

components.body: Select the primary purpose for the insulated rescue hook.

_items.text: To be used by suitably trained staff in an emergency to remove a casualty from an electric shock source
To be used by the workshop manager to drag a technician away from a shock source
To move high-voltage cables away from the car if they become short-circuited

_feedback.correct: Correct! The rescue hook is for trained staff to safely remove a casualty from a shock source.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. The rescue hook is for suitably trained staff to remove a casualty from an electric shock source.

components.body: Select the primary purpose for the insulated gloves — Class 0 rated 1500V DC.

_items.text: Keeps hands clean and dry
Prevents the spread of infection
Should be worn by suitably trained persons when working on the High Voltage system

_feedback.correct: Correct! These specialist gloves protect against high-voltage shock during HV system work.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. These Class 0 rated gloves are specifically for HV system work by trained technicians.

components.body: Select the primary purpose for the warning signs.

_items.text: Alerts others to the risks that are present
Stops anyone moving the vehicle from the workbay
Prevents access to the workbay

_feedback.correct: Correct! Warning signs alert others to the specific risks present.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. The primary purpose of warning signs is to alert others to risks.

components.body: Select the primary purpose for the vehicle status signs.

_items.text: Tells staff that the vehicle might need charging
Placed on the vehicle to let everyone know the condition and operational status
Allows the service advisor to find their cars easily

_feedback.correct: Correct! Status signs communicate the vehicle's condition and operational status.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. Vehicle status signs let everyone know the current condition and operational status.

components.displayTitle: Equipment Check: Do Not Start Sign
components.body: Select the primary purpose for the 'Do Not Start / Work in Progress' sign.

_items.text: Should be used to prevent damage or danger caused by unauthorised starting during repair
Should be used to save fuel by asking people not to start the vehicle
Should be used to keep the steering wheel clean

_feedback.correct: Correct! The sign prevents unauthorised starting that could cause damage or danger during repairs.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. This sign prevents unauthorised starting during the repair process.

components.body:
Understanding which risks relate to which vehicle systems is critical for safe working.

  • Ready to drive without running engine: Risk of unexpected movement
  • Silent electric motor operation: Pedestrians may not notice moving vehicles
  • Automatic stop-start systems: Vehicle may start unexpectedly
  • High-voltage components: Risk of electric shock from battery, cables, generator
components.imagePrompt: Electric vehicle risk assessment diagram showing warning zones around vehicle professional technical illustration dark background with amber and red highlights 800x600 no text

_graphic.alt: Risk assessment diagram for electric vehicle systems

components.body: For each identified risk, specific mitigation steps must be followed:

_items.title: Unexpected Movement
_items.body: Pay attention to instrument cluster warnings. Remove keys to prevent starting. Isolate HV and LV systems when working on the vehicle.

_items.title: Silent Operation Risk
_items.body: Be vigilant when driving EVs near people. Ensure AVAS is functioning correctly. Sound your horn when approaching pedestrians.

_items.title: Unexpected Engine Start
_items.body: Remove keys and use signal blocker pouch. Disconnect low-voltage system. Set out signage to let others know this is an EV.

_items.title: Electric Shock Prevention
_items.body: Isolate high-voltage system. Cordon off vehicles being worked on. Ensure only qualified persons work on the HV system.

components.body: To work safely around EV charging systems, you need to understand the basic electrical terminology. These concepts determine charging speed, cable ratings, and safety thresholds.
components.imagePrompt: Modern EV charging station with illuminated connector plugged into vehicle blue LED glow professional photography dark moody atmosphere 800x600 no text

_graphic.alt: Modern EV charging station with illuminated connector

components.instruction: Click each card to reveal the definition.

_items.back: Watts measure energy consumption — the rate at which electrical energy is used or generated. EV chargers are rated in kilowatts (kW).
Amps (amperes) measure electrical current — the flow of electricity through a circuit. A higher amp reading means more current is flowing.
Voltage is electrical pressure — the force that pushes current through a circuit. ICE vehicles use 12V, trucks 24V, but EVs operate at 350-800V DC.
Kilowatt-hours measure battery capacity. A 52kWh battery charging at 2.3kW takes about 22.6 hours. Typical EV batteries range from 40-100kWh.

_items.definition: A sudden release of electrical energy through the air when a high-voltage gap is breached. Can cause serious burns, injuries, or equipment damage.
Battery Electric Vehicle — powered entirely by a rechargeable battery pack with no internal combustion engine.
Hybrid Electric Vehicle — combines a conventional engine with an electric motor. Battery charges through regenerative braking only.
Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle — has both a combustion engine and electric motor with external charging capability.
Mild Hybrid Electric Vehicle — has a small electric motor that assists the combustion engine but cannot power the vehicle alone. Typically 48V.
The UN regulation defining high voltage as >60V to ≤1500V DC for electric vehicles. Specifies all required safety tests.
The main high-voltage battery providing power to the electric motor in an EV.
Converts high-voltage DC from the traction battery to low-voltage 12V DC for vehicle accessories.
An insulated tool for trained personnel to safely pull a person away from high-voltage components during electric shock.

components.body:
You have completed the EV Awareness & Safety course. Remember: always identify the vehicle type before beginning any work, never touch orange cables, and follow your organisation's specific EV safety procedures.

If you have any questions about working with electric vehicles, consult your workshop manager or a suitably trained EV specialist.
